children.text: Can you tell me a little about the schools you went to?

My mother would not send us to the Edgewood Schools because they were very poor. My mother was high on education probably because she didn't have access to an education. My father was going to night school. He worked full time. You know, and went to school at night. There were windows... You wanna talk about quality time- at that time there was no such thing. You didn'tt hear about those things. But, that was the quality time between 3 and 5, you know, two hours when my father came home from work, and getting ready to go to school. He went to St. Mary’s. The law school was downtown in the St. Mary’s church in the basement. I remember because my mom use to drop him off and we would go pick him up at night. And I remember because it was in the basement, and he used to come up from the basement. I was a kid. I was a little kid. Schools that we went to, and I think this goes back to- again my mom. She converted to the Catholic church when she married my dad. And they had to sign contracts back then that they were going to raise their kids Catholic, blah, blah, blah. My mom took that to mean that you are going to educate them as Catholics. So she sent us to private school. We went to Christ the King. That was on Perez St, also on the west side, with Cordi-Marian Sisters.
Are these sisters, are they predominantly Anglo?

They were predominantly Mexicanas. The way they came to San Antonio, they were fleeing the Revolution. Yes. Their mother house is in Chicago I believe, and they have a big I guess, convent, congregation here in San Antonio. At that time they had the school there on Perez St. That’s where David, myself, Johnny, Ger- all of us went there. And even my niece and nephew went there, and then they shut the school down. Obviously, because of economic reasons.

Yeah, I went there for – well... Here we go (laughter). Do you really want to know this? (laughter)- I went to kindergarten there, and I was kicked out of kindergarten because I didn't have the social skills, supposedly, to be in kindergarten. And my mother took me out. What it was, is they use to separate the boys and the girls. I grew up with boys. My cousins- they are all boys. The neighborhood I grew up with- in, they were all predominately boys. All my brother’s friends came over. They were my friends- they're still my friends. So, I did not know about not playing with boys, or that I “played like a boy,” you know. So, I was kind of aggressive and rough, and tough. The nuns didn’t like that. And so, my mom took me out. I don't even think I was there half a year. And I did not go back until I was five or six- until I went to the first grade. And then my little brother was there in kindergarten. They use to bring him across the street from kindergarten to first grade because he would cry.
What was- do you remember how the education was? Maybe you can also compare it to some of your friends that went to public school?

Oh, no. The education, I think we were pretty advanced. One of the things that I really resented was that they wanted us to speak English. These were nuns that could barely speak English themselves. And I’ll tell you because -Gosh. I hate to sound like a rat, you know, because nuns were pretty- they were nuns- they were angry. They were angry, too, you know. I am not sure- what they were angry at, other than they had to flee their home. But, they were so intimidated by supposedly teaching us English, you know, that they wouldn't allow us to speak Spanish. They would charge us, that’s how they would punish us, for every word we spoke in Spanish. Which, you know, to us was “Okay, we don’t have the money, but you can chalk it up on the bill, you know. Put it on my tab.” Because we did not have the money to pay you! But that’s what they did. And they set up a little court system. And they had like, spies that said “she spoke in Spanish- and she did this and he did that.”

Yeah! And so, once a week, every Friday, I’ll never forget. Right before school was let out we would go to the mock court. You know, and they would tell us all of our infractions, what we did wrong, and all this kind of stuff. Of course, I was always in court. Guess who the judge was? "Who was that?" My brother, David. So this was another tactic, you know brother against sister. (Unintelligable Question) Not really, he would go home and tell my mother. “They're embarrassing me. I am the oldest.” He was the oldest, you know, he was in charge of us. Making sure we stayed in line, and stuff like that. But it wasn’t working, obviously. But we would go home and he’d tell mom – du da duh. But, you know, my mom, she would tell us, “You better behave. La la la, la la la.” But at the same time she would tell my brother, "You're brothers and sisters, you're not going against each other." BLAH. PERIOD.

The Mexican Revolution took place from November 20th, 1910 to December 1st, 1920, with military generals holding power until 1940. It was a civil war that was characterized by regional conflicts, armed rebelions, and a number of unstable transitions of power after the long regime of Porfirio Díaz from 1876 to 1911. The Constitutionalist faction emerged victorious. They created the Constitution of 1917, which is still in use today. It was the first constitution of the world to incorporated labor rights and land reform, though not all succeeding leaders followed these aspects.

The Cordian-Mary Sisters were established in 1920 in Mexico City. They immigrated to the United States in 1926 when they faced religious persecution in Mexico, as the Mexican Revolution was secularist. The helped create the Christ the King parochial school that Montejano attended, which operated from 1944 until the mid 1980s, when it closed.

This was during the "English-Only" period of pedegaogy within the United States, when many states and schools had laws and policies that forbade the teaching of any other language except for English. It began during World War I as part of the Americanization Movement, and continued until the passing of the Bilingual Education Act in 1968. In most cases, children were also not allowed to speak any other language except for English anywhere at school, even in the cafeterias or the playgrounds. Punishments varied across the state. While Christ the King had this "court system" in place, schools led by Anglo staff had far more severe punishments, ranging from detentions and suspensions to ear-pinchings and paddlings.
